title: The unequal effects of the health–economy trade-off during the COVID-19 pandemic

data_scope.study_area: New York–Newark–Jersey City, NY–NJ–PA Metropolitan Statistical Area (FIPS C3562)
data_scope.mobility_data_provider: Cuebiq (opted-in GPS users; exact count not stated in article)

core_conclusion: Mandated non-pharmaceutical interventions and spontaneous fear-driven behavioural change produce qualitatively similar health–economy trade-offs: both increase unemployment and reduce COVID-19 deaths. Low-income workers in in-person, customer-facing occupations bear the sharpest trade-off. Closing only customer-facing industries achieves nearly the same death reduction as closing all non-essential activities while reducing unemployment by 36%.
why_it_matters: Quantitatively resolves the NPI-versus-behavioural-change debate; mechanistically explains income-stratified health and economic disparities; identifies customer-facing industry closure as an efficient targeted policy; shows that a 2-week delay in protective measures inflates deaths by 50% for only a 2% reduction in unemployment; provides an open Zenodo-archived ABM codebase.
why_nature: Behaviour-centred coupling mechanism targets Nature Human Behaviour scope; combines computational social science with public health in a validated multi-disciplinary framework; counterfactual causal reasoning constitutes methodological best practice; findings have direct welfare implications for pandemic preparedness policy.
added_value_to_my_research: Bipartite venue-mobility contact network → couples GPS-trajectory KDE with epidemic risk in telehealth/digital-divide settings
Neural surrogate of the ABM → physics-informed graph learner for real-time policy optimization of health–economy trade-offs
Job–housing commuter graph augmentation → network community detection identifies which spatial clusters bear greatest health–economy exposure, enriching 2SFCA catchment weighting
Occupation/information-access-heterogeneous fear model → mechanistic foundation linking digital-divide and health-information access to spatial health outcome disparities
Multi-city generalization via mobility-driven synthetic contact matrices → cross-city urban-scaling analysis linking labour-market structure to trade-off curve steepness

related.title: Mobility network models of COVID-19 explain inequities and inform reopening
related.relation: Full-resolution mobility network replaces aggregate contact weights; directly shows neighbourhood-level inequality in infection risk

related.title: Machine learning for data-centric epidemic forecasting
related.relation: Neural surrogate/emulator approach to replace expensive ABC calibration; enables real-time policy optimization

related.title: Behavioral changes during the COVID-19 pandemic decreased income diversity of urban encounters
related.relation: Quantifies income-segregating effect of pandemic mobility restrictions; complements the paper's income-stratified unemployment and infection findings

related.title: Modelling the impact of testing, contact tracing and household quarantine on second waves of COVID-19
related.relation: Layers targeted behavioural interventions (testing/tracing) on mobility-based contact networks; extends the paper's NPI counterfactual space

related.title: Mobility-driven synthetic contact matrices as a scalable solution for real-time pandemic response modeling
related.relation: Provides the scalable mobility-proxy infrastructure needed to generalize the paper's single-city ABM to multi-city comparison

figures.caption: Schematic representation of the joint epidemic–economic model showing the input–output network, four-layer contact network, and agent socioeconomic heterogeneity
Model validation against the first COVID-19 wave in New York — economic statistics, death counts, industry-level employment, and community contact patterns
Aggregate counterfactual results: deaths versus unemployment across fear-of-infection levels, closure scopes, and intervention timing
Counterfactuals disaggregated by income class (workplace infections and unemployment), census-tract unemployment maps, and industry-specific infection/consumption results
Age-specific fear of infection results: aggregate outcomes, contact/consumption time series by age group, and cross-age infection and demand dynamics
Full methodology schematic — data inputs/outputs for both modules, synthetic agent attributes, epidemic compartment diagram (SLPIR), and economic causal diagram
